---
title: Contributing
toc: true
toc-title: "On this page"
toc-location: left
---

## Overview

Thanks for showing interest in contributing to `pyfixest`! We appreciate all
contributions and constructive feedback, whether that be reporting bugs, requesting
new features, or suggesting improvements to documentation.

If you'd like to get involved, but are not yet sure how, please feel free to send us an [email](alexander-fischer1801@t-online.de). Some familiarity with
either Python or econometrics will help, but you really don't need to be a `numpy` core developer or have published in [Econometrica](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/14680262) =) We'd be more than happy to invest time to help you get started!

::: {.callout-note}
## PyFixest Sprint in Heilbronn

We're hosting a [PyFixest Sprint](pyfixest-sprint.md) with [AppliedAI](https://www.appliedai.de/) in late February/early March 2026. If you're interested in contributing to PyFixest in person, [learn more and get in touch](pyfixest-sprint.md)!
:::

For a comprehensive overview of the codebase architecture and internals, check out the [DeepWiki](https://deepwiki.com/py-econometrics/pyfixest). While not perfect and correct in all regards, we think it is a pretty good starting point to learn about the codebase!

## Quick Start with GitHub Codespaces

If you are new to open source / Python development, the fastest way to start contributing might be with **GitHub Codespaces**.
You start by forking the repository. You can then launch a codespace by clicking

[![Open in Codespaces](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open%20in-Codespaces-blue?logo=github)](https://github.com/py-econometrics/pyfixest/codespaces)

This will launch a github codespace (there is a free tier for 60h a month, thank you github!).

```{.bash .code-copy}
# install dependencies and compile the Rust extension
pixi install
```

Now, create a new Python script `debug.py` at the root of the repository and
paste the following:

```{python}
import pyfixest as pf

data = pf.get_data()
fit = pf.feols("Y ~ X1", data=data)
```

You can now run the script by typing

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi run python3 debug.py
```

or run unit tests

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi run test-default
```

## Reporting bugs

We use [GitHub issues](https://github.com/py-econometrics/pyfixest/issues) to track bugs. You can report a bug by opening a new issue or contribute to an existing issue if
related to the bug you are reporting.

Before creating a bug report, please check that your bug has not already been reported, and that your bug exists on the latest version of pyfixest. If you find a closed issue that seems to report the same bug you're experiencing, open a new issue and include a link to the original issue in your issue description.

Please include as many details as possible in your bug report. The information helps the maintainers resolve the issue faster.

## Suggesting enhancements

We use [GitHub issues](https://github.com/py-econometrics/pyfixest/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3Aenhancement) to track bugs and suggested enhancements. You can suggest an enhancement by opening a new feature request. Before creating an enhancement suggestion, please check that a similar issue does not already exist.

Please describe the behavior you want and why, and provide examples of how pyfixest would be used if your feature were added.

## Contributing to the codebase

### Setting up your local environment

First, fork the pyfixest GitHub repository, then clone your fork:

```{.bash .code-copy}
git clone https://github.com/<username>/pyfixest.git
cd pyfixest
```

### Package Management via `pixi`

`PyFixest` uses [pixi](https://pixi.sh/latest/) for package management. All configuration lives in `pyproject.toml` under `[tool.pixi.*]` sections.

To install `pixi`, follow the [installation instructions](https://pixi.sh/latest/#installation). Then:

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi install
```

This installs the default environment with all Python dependencies and builds the Rust extension via the editable install. You do not need a global Rust installation -- Rust is installed via conda.

After installation, you can activate the environment:

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi shell
```

### Running tests

```{.bash .code-copy}
# quick test suite (no R, no extended, no plots, no HAC)
pixi run test-default

# extended test suite
pixi run test-extended

# HAC standard error tests (runs with pinned thread counts)
pixi run test-hac
```

To run tests that compare against R's `fixest`, use the `r` environment:

```{.bash .code-copy}
# tests against R's fixest (core R packages from conda-forge)
pixi run -e r test-r-core

# tests against R's fixest (extra R packages from CRAN)
pixi run -e r test-r-extended

# run test_vs_fixest.py specifically
pixi run -e r test-vs-fixest

# run everything (all markers, all tests)
pixi run -e r test-all
```

### Linting

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi run -e lint pre-commit
```

### Building the documentation

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi run -e docs docs-build
pixi run -e docs docs-render
pixi run -e docs docs-preview
```

## Developer guide

This section explains the packaging setup, pixi environments, and automation
so you understand how the pieces fit together.

### Environments

All pixi configuration lives in `pyproject.toml`. There are four environments:

| Environment | Command | What it contains |
|---|---|---|
| **default** | `pixi install` | Python, Rust, test tooling, pyfixest (editable install). Works on all platforms including Windows. |
| **r** | `pixi install -e r` | Everything in default + R, rpy2, and R packages (fixest, sandwich, broom, etc.). Linux and macOS only -- R dependencies cannot be resolved on Windows via conda-forge. |
| **docs** | `pixi install -e docs` | Everything in default + quartodoc, quarto, jupyterlab, etc. |
| **lint** | `pixi install -e lint` | Only pre-commit. Does not include pyfixest or Rust (`no-default-feature = true`). |

The `r` environment has its own solve-group (separate from default) because it
is restricted to non-Windows platforms. This means package versions in `r` may
differ slightly from default, but this has no practical impact on testing.

### Rust extension and the maturin import hook

pyfixest includes a Rust extension (`pyfixest.core._core_impl`) built via
[maturin](https://www.maturin.rs/). The build system is declared in
`pyproject.toml` under `[build-system]`.

**Initial build:** The editable install (`pyfixest = { path = ".", editable = true }`)
triggers maturin automatically during `pixi install`. No manual build step is needed.

**Rebuilds after `.rs` changes:** We use
[maturin-import-hook](https://github.com/PyO3/maturin-import-hook) to
automatically rebuild the Rust extension whenever you `import pyfixest` and
the `.rs` source files have changed. The hook is installed via the `setup`
task, which runs automatically as a dependency of all test tasks (cached after
first run). You never need to manually rebuild.

How this works under the hood:

1. The `setup` task runs `python -m maturin_import_hook site install`, which
   writes a `sitecustomize.py` into the environment that activates the hook on
   every Python startup.
2. The task creates a sentinel file (`.pixi/.import-hook-installed`) and
   declares it as an `output`, so pixi caches the result and skips re-running
   on subsequent invocations.
3. When Python starts and you `import pyfixest`, the hook checks whether any
   `.rs` files are newer than the compiled extension. If so, it runs
   `maturin develop --release --strip --uv` automatically.

::: {.callout-note}
If you run `pixi install` again (e.g. after a lock file update), the
environment is recreated and the sentinel file is removed. The next test run
will re-run `setup` automatically.
:::

### Task dependencies and caching

Several tasks use pixi's `depends-on`, `inputs`, and `outputs` to avoid
redundant work:

- **`setup`** (maturin import hook): runs once, cached via sentinel file.
  All test tasks in the default environment depend on it.
- **`update-test-data`**: runs `Rscript tests/r_test_comparisons.R` to
  generate `tests/data/ritest_results.csv`. Cached via `inputs`/`outputs` --
  only re-runs if the R script changes or the CSV is missing. The R test tasks
  (`test-r-core`, `test-r-extended`, `test-vs-fixest`, `test-all`) depend on it.
- **`install-r-extended`**: installs extra R packages from CRAN that are not
  available on conda-forge. Cached via sentinel file. Only `test-r-extended`
  depends on it (the other R tests only need conda-forge R packages).

You can skip dependency tasks with `--skip-deps` if needed:

```{.bash .code-copy}
pixi run -e r --skip-deps test-r-core
```

### All available tasks

| Task | Environment | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `setup` | default | Install the maturin import hook (auto-runs before tests) |
| `test-default` | default | Quick tests (no R, extended, plots, or HAC) |
| `test-extended` | default | Tests marked `extended` |
| `test-hac` | default | HAC SE tests (with pinned thread counts) |
| `test-all` | r | All tests including R comparisons |
| `test-r-core` | r | Tests against R's fixest (conda-forge R packages) |
| `test-r-extended` | r | Tests against R (extra CRAN packages) |
| `test-vs-fixest` | r | Runs `test_vs_fixest.py` specifically |
| `update-test-data` | r | Regenerate R test comparison data |
| `install-r-extended` | r | Install extra R packages from CRAN |
| `render-notebooks` | default | Run all notebooks via `scripts/run_notebooks.py` |
| `docs-build` | docs | Build API docs with quartodoc |
| `docs-render` | docs | Render docs with quarto |
| `docs-preview` | docs | Preview docs locally |
| `pre-commit` | lint | Run all pre-commit hooks |

### Why rpy2 is pinned

`rpy2` is pinned to `==3.5.11` because newer versions broke pandas
DataFrame conversion with R. This pin also prevents the `r` environment from
resolving on Windows (rpy2 3.5.11 on Windows only supports Python 3.8 on
conda-forge). Unpinning requires testing the full R test suite against a
newer rpy2 version.

## Installing Python, R and Quarto

#### Installing Python

The minimal Python version to develop `pyfixest` is `3.10`. You can install it on Mac/Linux via [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/):

```{.bash .code-copy}
brew install python@3.11 # specify the version of python you prefer
```

On Windows via [Winget](https://winget.run/pkg/Python/Python.3.11):
```{.bash .code-copy}
winget install -e --id Python.Python.3.11
```

### Installing R

R and R dependencies available through conda-forge are installed automatically when you use the `r` environment (`pixi install -e r`). You do not need a global R installation.

Some extra R dependencies (installed via `pixi run -e r install-r-extended`) may require additional development tools like `gcc` and `cmake`.

### Installing Quarto

Quarto is installed automatically in the `docs` environment via conda. You do not need a global Quarto installation to build the documentation.
